content.full_text:
MEMBERS
BILL FILANTE,
Vice Charr
JACK O'CONNELI.
Majority Vice Chair
'. ... ,\
LUCY KILLEA 
\I"'.
RICHARD MOUNTJOY
PAT NOLAN
RICHARD POTANCO
JACKIE SPEIER
CATHIE WRIGHT
@alilur niu ptgixlallur e
$***mhlg prules @rrmmillee
ROOM 3016 - STATE CAPITOT
P.O BOX 942849
SACRA['ENTO, CALIFORNIA 94249-OOO ]
TELEPHONE: {91 6) 445-81 73
TOM BANE
CHAIRMAN
March L4, 1990
!tr. John wallace
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J street
sacramento, cA 95814
Dear John:
I am writing to confirm telephone advice that you gave to me
today on the interpretation of the transfer and the trust
provisions of Proposition 73.
You advised me that the follolring expenditures by a candidate
yrould constitute independent expenditures, and would be
prohibited by the transfer ban in Section 85304.
You also advised me that these expenditures would not violate
the trust provision of Section 85202. Section 85801 provides
that expenditures to seek office are within the Iawful
execution of the trust if they are reasonably related to a
political pulpose. You agreed that the expenditures described
are reasonably related to the candidate's political 
purpose of
seeking election.
1. Expenditures for a slate mailer which includes the
candidate, and which also includes other candidates, do not
contribute to the slate mailer, and who have not in any way
been involved in or authorized the mailer. The reason for
inclusion of the other candidates is to lend credibility 
to
the slate mailer, and thus to increase its effectiveness as
a mailer for the candidate'
You advised that the pro-rata cost of the mailer attributed to
the other candidates would be considered to be an independent
expenditure, and would thus be prohibited.
CHIEF ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICEB
BOB CONNELLY
EXECUTIVE SECRETARY
TERRIE F, WLFONG


llr. John WaLLace
Page E\do
March 14, 1990
2. Expenditures for a campaign mailer which includes a
photograph of the candidate with another candidate (such as
a candidate for Governor or other statewide office). 
The
other candidate has had no invoLvement with the mailer.
The purpose of the photograph is to associate the candidate
in the eyes of the voters with the other candidate, and to
get the benefit of the good will and support for the other
candidate.
You advised thar- the pro-rata cost of inclusion of the other
candidate's photograph would constitute an independent
expenditure to the other candidate, and would be prohibited.
3. Expenditures for a canpaign maifer which includes the
endorsement of another candidate, such as a candidate for
Governor. The other candidate has had no involvement in
the mail-er. The purpose of the endorsement is to convince
voters who support the candidate making the endorsement to
vote for the candidate sending the mailer.
You advised that the pro-rata cost of the endorsement would be
an independent expenditure for the candidate making the
endorsement, and would be prohibited.
4. Expenditures for a mailer that incfude the photograph, or a
reference to, the candidate's opponent, The opponent has
had no involvement in the mailer. The purpose of the photo
or reference to the opponent is to convince voters not to
vote for the opponent (although for some voters the actual
effect could be the opposite).
You advised that the pro-rata cost of the photo or reference
would be an independent expenditure for the opponent, and would
be prohibited.
You also advised me that if a reference to another candidate
appears in a slate mailer or campaign mai]er sent by a
candidate, and the mailer has been authorized by or coordinated
with the other candidate, the other candidate must pay his or
her pro-rata share of the mailing. 
Such payments must be made
directly to the vendors in order to avoid a transfer. 
However,
if such direct pal.ments are made, there is no contribution to,
or independent expenditure for, the other candidatei and there
is no violation of the transfer ban.
As a staff person in the Assenbly with familiarity erith the
Political Reform Act, I am frequently asked to explain the Act,
and to advise and assist on legislation involving the Act. It


!1r. John wallace
Page Three
March J-4, 1990
would be a great hefp to me if you would either confirm this
telephone advice promptly, or te1I me if you are planning to
change your advice .
S incerely,
BIrI : bnl
Barbara l.1i1man
Staff Counsel

sections.question_synthetic: The document is a staff member's letter requesting confirmation of FPPC telephone advice regarding whether certain candidate expenditures (slate mailers, photos with other candidates, endorsements, and references to opponents) constitute prohibited independent expenditures or violations under Proposition 73's transfer and trust provisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is incoming correspondence from a non-FPPC staff member requesting confirmation of prior oral FPPC advice, not an FPPC advice letter response. Contains detailed recitation of telephone advice given regarding four types of campaign expenditures and coordination scenarios. Document lacks standard Q/A section structure typical of formal FPPC advice letters.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The document is a staff member's letter requesting confirmation of FPPC telephone advice regarding whether certain candidate expenditures (slate mailers, photos with other candidates, endorsements, and references to opponents) constitute prohibited independent expenditures or violations under Proposition 73's transfer and trust provisions.

embedding.summary: A California Assembly staff counsel writes to confirm telephone advice previously given by FPPC Legal Division regarding interpretation of Proposition 73's transfer ban and trust provisions, specifically addressing whether certain candidate mailer expenditures constitute prohibited independent expenditures.
